sections.title: Listening
sections.description: 40 questions • Approximately 30 minutes • You will hear each recording only once

parts.title: Part 1: Conversation between two people in an everyday social context
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 1-10.

script.narrator:
Good morning, this is the International English Language Testing System, IELTS. You will hear a number of different recordings and you will have to answer questions on what you hear. There will be time for you to read the instructions and questions and you will have a chance to check your work. All the recordings will be played once only. The test is in four parts. At the end of the test you will be given ten minutes to transfer your answers to an answer sheet.

Now turn to Part 1. You will hear a conversation between two people in an everyday social context. Look at questions 1 to 10. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 10.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: HHHHHHHH Good morning, how can I help you today?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Good morning, I'd like to book a hotel room for next week, please.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Certainly, sir. What dates are you looking for?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I need a room from Monday the 15th to Friday the 19th.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Let me check availability for you. Yes, we have rooms available. What type of room would you prefer?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: I'd like a single room with a sea view if possible.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I can offer you a single room with sea view for 120 pounds per night.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That sounds reasonable. Does that include breakfast?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Yes, continental breakfast is included. May I take your name and contact details?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Of course, my name is David Thompson, that's D-A-V-I-D T-H-O-M-P-S-O-N.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Thank you, Mr. Thompson. And your phone number?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Perfect. I'll send you a confirmation email. Is there anything else I can help you with?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Actually, yes. What time is check-in and check-out?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Check-in is from 2 PM and check-out is by 11 AM. Is there anything else?

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: No, that's everything. Thank you very much for your help.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: You're welcome, Mr. Thompson. Have a wonderful stay with us.

questions.questionText: What is the man's name?
questions.translation: Tên của người đàn ông là gì?

questions.questionText: Where does the woman work?
questions.translation: Người phụ nữ làm việc ở đâu?

questions.questionText: What time does the meeting start?
questions.translation: Cuộc họp bắt đầu lúc mấy giờ?

questions.questionText: How many people are expected?
questions.translation: Dự kiến có bao nhiêu người?

questions.questionText: What is the main topic?
questions.translation: Chủ đề chính là gì?

questions.questionText: Which room is booked?
questions.translation: Phòng nào đã được đặt?

questions.questionText: What is the phone number?
questions.translation: Số điện thoại là gì?

questions.questionText: When is the deadline?
questions.translation: Hạn chót là khi nào?

questions.questionText: Who is the speaker?
questions.translation: Người nói là ai?

questions.questionText: What is the purpose?
questions.translation: Mục đích là gì?

parts.title: Part 2: Monologue in an everyday social context
parts.instructions: Listen to the monologue and answer questions 11-20.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 2. You will hear a monologue in an everyday social context. Look at questions 11 to 20. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 20.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: Welcome to the National History Museum. I'm Sarah, your guide for today's tour. The museum was established in 1923 and has been showcasing artifacts from around the world for over a century. Our most popular exhibit is the Egyptian collection, which features several mummies and artifacts from ancient tombs. The collection was expanded in 2000 with a special donation from the British Museum. We welcome over one million visitors annually, making us one of the most visited museums in the country. Adult admission is ten pounds, and we're open every day except Monday. The museum opens at 10 AM and closes at 6 PM. Our gift shop is located on the ground floor near the main entrance. This evening, we have a special lecture on ancient civilizations at 7 PM in the main auditorium.

questions.questionText: What is the main subject of the talk?
questions.translation: Chủ đề chính của bài nói là gì?

questions.questionText: How long has the museum been open?
questions.translation: Bảo tàng đã mở cửa được bao lâu?

questions.questionText: What is the most popular exhibit?
questions.translation: Triển lãm nào phổ biến nhất?

questions.questionText: When was the special collection added?
questions.translation: Bộ sưu tập đặc biệt được thêm vào khi nào?

questions.questionText: How many visitors come annually?
questions.translation: Có bao nhiêu lượt khách tham quan hàng năm?

questions.questionText: What is the admission fee for adults?
questions.translation: Phí vào cửa cho người lớn là bao nhiêu?

questions.questionText: Which day is the museum closed?
questions.translation: Bảo tàng đóng cửa vào ngày nào?

questions.questionText: What time does the museum open?
questions.translation: Bảo tàng mở cửa lúc mấy giờ?

questions.questionText: Where is the gift shop located?
questions.translation: Cửa hàng quà tặng nằm ở đâu?

questions.questionText: What special event is mentioned?
questions.translation: Sự kiện đặc biệt nào được đề cập?

parts.title: Part 3: Conversation between up to four people in an educational or training context
parts.instructions: Listen to the conversation and answer questions 21-30.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 3. You will hear a conversation between up to four people in an educational or training context. Look at questions 21 to 30. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 30.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Today we're discussing renewable energy sources and their impact on the environment. Sarah, what do you think is the biggest challenge facing renewable energy adoption?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I think the main challenge is cost, Professor. Initial setup costs can be quite high, even though the long-term benefits are significant.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: That's a valid point. Currently, about 25% of our energy comes from renewable sources. Which renewable source do you think is most efficient?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I believe hydropower is the most efficient because it can generate electricity consistently and has a high energy conversion rate.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Interesting perspective. However, I think the timeline for achieving 50% renewable energy might be too optimistic. What's your view on this?

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: I think 15 to 20 years is realistic if governments invest properly in infrastructure and technology.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: The main advantage, as you mentioned, is the cleaner environment. Denmark is a great example - they generate over 50% of their electricity from wind power.

conversation.speaker: female
conversation.text: That's impressive. I think the key to success is having skilled practitioners who can implement these technologies effectively.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Exactly. Now, let's move on to discussing the economic implications of this transition.

questions.questionText: What is the main topic of discussion?
questions.translation: Chủ đề chính của cuộc thảo luận là gì?

questions.questionText: According to the professor, what is the biggest challenge?
questions.translation: Theo giáo sư, thách thức lớn nhất là gì?

questions.questionText: What percentage of energy comes from renewables?
questions.translation: Bao nhiêu phần trăm năng lượng đến từ nguồn tái tạo?

questions.questionText: Which renewable source is most efficient?
questions.translation: Nguồn tái tạo nào hiệu quả nhất?

questions.questionText: What does Sarah think about the timeline?
questions.translation: Sarah nghĩ gì về thời gian biểu?

questions.questionText: How many years does the professor predict?
questions.translation: Giáo sư dự đoán bao nhiêu năm?

questions.questionText: What is the main advantage mentioned?
questions.translation: Lợi ích chính được đề cập là gì?

questions.questionText: Which country is used as an example?
questions.translation: Quốc gia nào được sử dụng làm ví dụ?

questions.questionText: What does the professor recommend?
questions.translation: Giáo sư khuyến nghị gì?

questions.questionText: What is the next step according to Sarah?
questions.translation: Bước tiếp theo theo Sarah là gì?

parts.title: Part 4: Monologue on an academic subject
parts.instructions: Listen to the monologue and answer questions 31-40.

script.narrator:
Now turn to Part 4. You will hear a monologue on an academic subject. Look at questions 31 to 40. You now have some time to look at the questions.

[Pause]

Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

conversation.speaker: male
conversation.text: Today's lecture focuses on machine learning, a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn from data without explicit programming. Machine learning was first developed in the 1950s, marking a significant milestone in computer science. Supervised learning involves training algorithms with labeled examples, where the system learns to make predictions based on input-output pairs. Decision trees are particularly effective for classification tasks, as they can handle both numerical and categorical data. Typically, we use 80% of our data for training and reserve 20% for testing. The main challenge in machine learning is ensuring data quality, as poor data leads to unreliable models. Healthcare is the industry that benefits most from machine learning applications, particularly in diagnostic imaging and drug discovery. Deep learning uses multiple neural layers to process complex patterns in data. I predict that machine learning development will accelerate significantly in the coming years, driven by advances in computing power and data availability. The key to success in machine learning is having skilled practitioners who understand both the technical aspects and the domain knowledge required for effective implementation.

questions.questionText: What is the main subject of the lecture?
questions.translation: Chủ đề chính của bài giảng là gì?

questions.questionText: When was machine learning first developed?
questions.translation: Học máy được phát triển lần đầu khi nào?

questions.questionText: What is supervised learning?
questions.translation: Học có giám sát là gì?

questions.questionText: Which algorithm is mentioned for classification?
questions.translation: Thuật toán nào được đề cập cho phân loại?

questions.questionText: What percentage of data is typically used for training?
questions.translation: Bao nhiêu phần trăm dữ liệu thường được sử dụng để huấn luyện?

questions.questionText: What is the main challenge in machine learning?
questions.translation: Thách thức chính trong học máy là gì?

questions.questionText: Which industry benefits most from ML?
questions.translation: Ngành nào được hưởng lợi nhiều nhất từ ML?

questions.questionText: What is deep learning?
questions.translation: Học sâu là gì?

questions.questionText: What does the lecturer predict for the future?
questions.translation: Giảng viên dự đoán gì cho tương lai?

questions.questionText: What is the key to success in ML?
questions.translation: Chìa khóa thành công trong ML là gì?

passages.title: Passage 1: The History of Coffee
passages.content: Coffee cultivation first began in Ethiopia. Legend has it that a goat herder named Kaldi noticed his goats became energetic after eating berries from a certain tree. He tried the berries himself and felt a similar invigorating effect. Monks in a local monastery initially disapproved but later found that the berries helped them stay awake during long prayers. From Ethiopia, coffee spread to the Arabian Peninsula, where it was widely cultivated and traded. By the 15th century, coffee was being grown in Yemen, and by the 16th century, it had reached Persia, Egypt, Syria, and Turkey. Coffee houses, known as 'kaveh kanes,' became centers for social interaction, discussion, and entertainment. The drink eventually made its way to Europe in the 17th century, initially facing skepticism but quickly gaining popularity. The first coffee house in England opened in Oxford in 1650. Today, coffee is one of the most popular beverages worldwide, with a vast global industry supporting millions of farmers and workers across the globe. The coffee trade has shaped economies, influenced social customs, and even played a role in historical events such as the Boston Tea Party, where coffee became the preferred beverage of American revolutionaries.

questions.questionText: According to legend, who first discovered the invigorating effects of coffee berries?
Where did coffee cultivation first begin?
What were coffee houses known as in the Arabian Peninsula?
When did coffee reach Europe?
The first coffee house in England opened in which city?
What role did coffee play in the Boston Tea Party?
options: A) It was banned by the British
B) It became the preferred drink of revolutionaries
C) It was used as a substitute for tea

questions.questionText: How did monks initially react to coffee?
options: C) They used it for religious ceremonies

questions.questionText: By the 16th century, coffee had reached which regions?
options: B) Persia, Egypt, Syria, and Turkey

questions.questionText: What made coffee houses important in society?
options: A) They served the best coffee
B) They were centers for social interaction
C) They were cheaper than other establishments

questions.questionText: What is the main impact of the coffee industry today?
options: B) It supports millions of workers globally
C) It is declining in popularity
D) It is limited to Europe and America

passages.title: Passage 2: The Benefits of Renewable Energy
passages.content: Renewable energy sources, such as solar, wind, hydro, and geothermal, are becoming increasingly vital in the global effort to combat climate change and reduce reliance on fossil fuels. Unlike coal, oil, and natural gas, which are finite and produce harmful greenhouse gas emissions when burned, renewable sources are naturally replenished and have a significantly lower environmental impact. Solar power harnesses energy from the sun using photovoltaic panels or concentrated solar power systems. Wind power utilizes wind turbines to convert wind into electricity. Hydropower generates electricity by harnessing the energy of flowing water, typically through dams. Geothermal energy taps into the Earth's internal heat. The benefits of renewable energy extend beyond environmental protection; they also include energy independence, job creation in green industries, and reduced health costs associated with air pollution. While initial setup costs can be higher for some renewable projects, the long-term operational costs are often lower, and the fuel source is free. Countries like Denmark and Germany have successfully integrated renewable energy into their national grids, with Denmark generating over 50% of its electricity from wind power. The transition to renewable energy is not just an environmental necessity but also an economic opportunity that can create millions of new jobs while reducing our carbon footprint.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a renewable energy source?
What is a key difference between fossil fuels and renewable energy sources?
options: A) Fossil fuels are cheaper to extract
C) Fossil fuels produce greenhouse gases
D) Renewable sources are less efficient

questions.questionText: How does hydropower generate electricity?
options: C) By tapping Earth's internal heat
D) By converting wind into electricity

questions.questionText: Which of these is a benefit of renewable energy mentioned in the text?
options: B) Increased reliance on fossil fuels
C) Job creation in green industries

questions.questionText: What is a potential drawback of some renewable energy projects?
options: B) Their fuel source is not free
C) Initial setup costs can be higher
D) They are not naturally replenished

questions.questionText: Which country generates over 50% of its electricity from wind power?
What does the text say about the fuel source for renewable energy?
What is geothermal energy?
options: C) Energy from Earth's internal heat

questions.questionText: What does the text suggest about the transition to renewable energy?
options: A) It is only an environmental necessity
B) It is only an economic opportunity
C) It is both environmental necessity and economic opportunity
D) It is neither necessary nor beneficial

questions.questionText: What can renewable energy help reduce?
options: C) Health costs from air pollution

passages.title: Passage 3: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Society
passages.content: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming various aspects of society, from healthcare and transportation to education and entertainment. AI refers to the simulation of human intelligence in machines that are programmed to think like humans and mimic their actions. The development of machine learning, a subset of AI, has enabled systems to learn from data without explicit programming, leading to breakthroughs in areas like image recognition, natural language processing, and predictive analytics. In healthcare, AI assists in diagnosing diseases, personalizing treatment plans, and accelerating drug discovery. Autonomous vehicles, powered by AI, promise to revolutionize transportation by improving safety and efficiency. However, the rise of AI also presents challenges, including concerns about job displacement, ethical implications of autonomous decision-making, and the potential for bias in AI algorithms. Ensuring responsible development and deployment of AI will be crucial for maximizing its benefits while mitigating its risks to society. Recent studies show that AI could contribute up to $15.7 trillion to the global economy by 2030, but this growth must be managed carefully to ensure equitable distribution of benefits. The European Union has already implemented the AI Act, the world's first comprehensive AI regulation, setting standards for AI development and use. As AI continues to evolve, the need for interdisciplinary collaboration between technologists, ethicists, policymakers, and society at large becomes increasingly important.

questions.questionText: What is AI primarily defined as?
options: A) The study of human behavior
B) The simulation of human intelligence in machines

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a subset of AI that allows systems to learn from data?
In which area does AI assist in diagnosing diseases and personalizing treatment plans?
What is one challenge associated with the rise of AI?
What is crucial for maximizing AI's benefits and mitigating its risks?
options: B) Ensuring responsible development and deployment
D) Focusing only on job creation

questions.questionText: How much could AI contribute to the global economy by 2030?
What is the AI Act?
options: B) The world's first comprehensive AI regulation

questions.questionText: Which organization implemented the AI Act?
What does the text suggest about AI growth?
options: B) It must be managed carefully

questions.questionText: What type of collaboration is needed for AI development?

tasks.title: Writing Task 1
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. The chart below shows the percentage of total food produced that was wasted in five different countries between 2005 and 2015. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a graph, table, chart or diagram and asked to describe, summarize or explain the information in your own words. You may be asked to describe and explain data, describe the stages of a process, how something works or describe an object or event.

tasks.title: Writing Task 2
tasks.instructions: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Some people believe that the best way to improve public health is by increasing the number of sports facilities. Others argue that this is not enough and that other measures are required. Discuss both these views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.description: You will be presented with a point of view, argument or problem. You need to write a discursive essay in response. This means you must discuss the topic from different perspectives and include your own opinion.

parts.title: Speaking Part 1: Introduction and Interview (4-5 minutes)
parts.instructions: The examiner will ask you general questions about yourself and a range of familiar topics, such as home, family, work, studies, and interests.
parts.description: This part of the test focuses on your ability to communicate opinions and information on everyday topics and common experiences or situations by answering a range of questions.

questions: Can we start with your full name, please?
What should I call you?
Do you work or are you a student?
What do you do for a living?
Let's talk about your hometown. Where are you from?
What do you like most about your hometown?
Is there anything you dislike about it?
How has your hometown changed over the years?
Would you like to live there in the future?
Do you live in a house or an apartment?
Can you describe your home?
What's your favorite room in your home?
What would you like to change about your home?
What do you do for work?
Do you enjoy your job?
What are you studying?
Why did you choose this field of study?
What do you like most about your studies?
What do you like to do in your free time?
Do you have any hobbies?
How long have you been interested in this hobby?
What do you find most enjoyable about it?

parts.title: Speaking Part 2: Long Turn (3-4 minutes: 1 minute to prepare, 1-2 minutes to speak, 1 minute for examiner questions)
parts.instructions: You will be given a task card which asks you to talk about a particular topic. You will have one minute to prepare your talk and make some notes, and then you will speak for one to two minutes.
parts.description: This part of the test focuses on your ability to speak at length on a given topic, using appropriate language and organizing your ideas coherently.

cueCard.topic: Describe a place you would like to visit in the future.
cueCard.explanation: and explain why you would like to visit this place.

alternativeTopics.topic: Describe a memorable journey you have taken.
alternativeTopics.explanation: and explain why this journey was memorable for you.

alternativeTopics.topic: Describe a person who has influenced you.
alternativeTopics.explanation: and explain how they have influenced you.

alternativeTopics.topic: Describe a book you have read recently.
alternativeTopics.explanation: and explain why you would or wouldn't recommend it to others.

parts.title: Speaking Part 3: Discussion (4-5 minutes)
parts.instructions: The examiner will ask further questions which are connected to the topic of Part 2. These questions will allow you to discuss more abstract ideas and issues.
parts.description: This part of the test focuses on your ability to express and justify opinions and to analyze, discuss and speculate about issues.

questions: What are some popular tourist destinations in your country?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of international tourism?
How important is it for people to learn about other cultures?
Do you think tourism will increase or decrease in the future?
What impact does tourism have on the environment?
How has technology changed the way people travel?
What are the benefits of traveling for young people?
Do you think governments should invest more in tourism?
How important is education in your country?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of online learning?
Do you think traditional classroom learning is still relevant?
How has technology changed education?
What skills do you think are most important for students to learn?
Do you think education should be free for everyone?
How can governments improve the quality of education?
What role should parents play in their children's education?
How has the job market changed in recent years?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of working from home?
Do you think job security is important?
How important is work-life balance?
What skills do you think will be most valuable in the future?
Do you think people should change jobs frequently?
How can companies attract and retain good employees?
What impact does automation have on employment?
How has society changed in your country over the past decade?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of globalization?
How important is it to preserve traditional culture?
Do you think social media has a positive or negative impact on society?
How can governments address social inequality?
What role should the media play in society?
How has technology affected social relationships?
Do you think people are becoming more or less social?

metadata.author: IELTS Practice System
metadata.notes: Comprehensive IELTS test with all four sections: Listening (4 parts, 40 questions), Reading (3 passages, 30 questions), Writing (2 tasks), and Speaking (3 parts), including dynamic voice profiles for realistic audio generation.
